summary: Fowler examines whether XP's emphasis on evolutionary design means abandoning traditional planned design. He argues that XP's enabling practices—testing, continuous integration, and refactoring—flatten the change curve enough to make evolutionary design viable, something impossible under traditional 'code and fix' approaches. The key insight is that design hasn't died but transformed: instead of big upfront design, XP enables a balance where refactoring and planning coexist. Fowler acknowledges this requires discipline, technical skill, and someone with 'the will to design' to prevent entropy.
key_points: Evolutionary design only works when enabled by XP practices like testing, continuous integration, and refactoring—without these, it devolves into code and fix chaos
YAGNI (You Aren't Going to Need It) is only valid advice when you have the enabling practices to change things cheaply later
The role of architect shifts from making all decisions to coaching developers and ensuring design quality is maintained
Simplicity is surprisingly hard to define—eliminating duplication is clear, but 'revealing intention' depends on the reader's experience level
Design reversibility is crucial: good designers think about how to defer decisions or make them easy to change later
money_quotes: Design is there to enable you to keep changing the software easily in the long term.
The fundamental assumption underlying XP is that it is possible to flatten the change curve enough to make evolutionary design work.
In XP 'clever code' is a term of abuse.
It's easier to refactor over-design than it is to refactor no design.
The best UML diagrams are not artifacts.
In XP technical leadership is shown by teaching the programmers and helping them make decisions.
If he doesn't trust his programmers why does he hire them?
In order to work, evolutionary design needs a force that drives it to converge. This force can only come from people.
key_insight: Evolutionary design isn't the absence of design—it's design enabled by practices that make change cheap, requiring even more skill and discipline than planned design.
